cards.text: Default member initialization: struct Point { int x {{c1::= 0}}; int y {{c2::= 0}}; };

cards.back:
Providing default values for struct members in the struct definition itself.
Example: struct Point { int x = 0; int y = 0; };

cards.question: What are p's values?
cards.code:
struct Point { int x = 5; int y = 10; };
Point p {};
cards.explanation: Empty braces use default member initializers.

cards.question: What are p's values?
cards.code:
struct Point { int x = 5; int y = 10; };
Point p { 20 };
cards.explanation: Explicit initializer (20) overrides default for x. y uses default.

cards.front: Do explicit initializers override default member initializers?
cards.back: Yes. Explicit initializers in aggregate initialization override defaults for those members.

cards.text: Default member initializers use {{c1::=}} or {{c2::brace}} initialization syntax.

cards.question: Are both initialization forms valid?
cards.answer: Yes. Both = and brace initialization are valid for default member initializers.
cards.explanation: Prefer braces for consistency with aggregate init.

cards.front: What are the benefits of default member initialization?
cards.back:
• Members always have valid values
  • Prevents uninitialized variables
  • Documents expected defaults
  • Reduces chance of bugs

cards.question: What are p's values?
cards.code:
struct Point { int x = 5; int y = 10; };
Point p;  // no initializer
cards.answer: x=5, y=10 (default member initializers are used)
cards.explanation: Even without explicit initialization, default member initializers apply.

cards.concept: When to use default member initialization
cards.explanation:
Use default member initialization when:
  • Most objects use the same default values
  • You want to ensure members are never uninitialized
  • The default is meaningful for the type
Best practice: provide defaults for all members when possible.
examples: struct Point { int x = 0; int y = 0; };

cards.text: Default member initializers are applied when a member is {{c1::not explicitly initialized}} during aggregate initialization.
Default member initializers can use {{c1::=}} or {{c2::brace}} initialization: int x {{c3::= 5}}; or int y {{c4::{ 10 }}};
Explicit initializers {{c1::override}} default member initializers during aggregate initialization.
Default member initializers can use {{c1::=}} or {{c2::brace}} initialization: int x {{c3::= 5}}; or int y {{c4::{ 10 }}};
